reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility of mechanistic interpretations (volatility ordering, LiPF6 decomposition products, humidity-mediated HF formation, and PF5 detectability) without requiring external inference beyond the stated scenario. Decoys are largely single-fault overclaims or mechanism eliminations that a domain expert would reject, creating meaningful discrimination. Slight reductions come from a few options leaning on absolute wording (“cannot,” “impossible,” “eliminates”) that can make elimination somewhat heuristic rather than purely mechanistic.

The item cleanly targets mechanistic boundary reasoning from TG/FTIR patterns (volatility staging, reactive intermediates, and moisture-limited secondary chemistry) and rewards avoiding common inference traps (non-detection ≠ absence; co-detection ≠ exclusivity). Decoys are engineering-plausible and hinge on single overclaim errors, creating meaningful separation for experts without relying on stylistic cues.

The prompt cleanly tests boundary-aware hazard reasoning: POF3 detection under humid conditions undermines the admissibility of a “no HF hazard” conclusion because HF can be generated post-release, so the missing variable is the environmental moisture context driving conversion. It has strong discriminative power because it penalizes snapshot measurement thinking and forces consideration of latent hazard formation from precursor species without requiring extraneous chemistry beyond that relationship.
